revision.comment: Sayjay1995 moved page [[User:Sayjay1995/sandbox]] to [[Wikipedia:Gunma-chan]]: I'm moving my sandbox article into Wikipedia. This is the English translation for an existing article.
Sayjay1995 moved page [[User:Sayjay1995/sandbox]] to [[Shorinzan Daruma Temple]]: It has significant cultural and regional importance.
Gathered English resources to support the information
revision.text:
Shorinzan Daruma Temple (少林山達磨寺) is a small temple located in [[Takasaki]], [[Gunma Prefecture]]. It was built in 1697 by the [[Obaku (school of Buddhism)|Obaku]] school of Zen Buddhism. Takasaki is known as the birthplace of [[Daruma doll|daruma]], as it is believed that the dolls originated from the region many years ago. The daruma of Shorinzan are said to be especially lucky, leading the city's PR department to dub Takasaki as The Lucky Town.<ref>{{cite web |title=The Lucky Town TAKASAKI |url=http://excitingcity-takasaki.com/ltt/index.html |website=excitingcity-takasaki.com |publisher=高崎市公式HP |access-date=6 June 2022}}</ref>
== Origin == An old legend states that sometime between 1673 and 1681, a heavy rain came and flooded the Usui River. Afterwards, villagers found a glowing, scented tree near the riverbed. They decided it was sacred and placed it inside an old hut that housed the local Kannon statue. As they did, purple haze began swirling around them.<ref name="DH1">{{cite web |title=【公式】黄檗宗 少林山達磨寺 |url=https://www.daruma.or.jp/ |website=www.daruma.or.jp |access-date=6 June 2022 |language=ja}}</ref>
Around the time of the flood, Bodhidharma Daishi appeared before a traveling monk named Ichiryo Koji in a dream. Alledgedly he commanded Ichiryo Koji to carve his likeness out of a tree that was exactly the height of his nose. In 1680 Ichiryo Koji began his search for that tree, finally finding it when he visited the village that had once flooded. Ichiryo Koji carved the Bodhidharma Daishi's likeness with great devotion and care; it was officially placed next to the Kannon statue, both enshrined together.<ref name="DH1" />
Ichiryo Koji's devotion inspired the local villagers, and after that the area became known as "Shorinzan", the sacred place where the Daruma appeared. The ruling lord of the time then had Shorinzan Daruma Temple founded in 1697 in order to protect his castle (historically, Maebashi Castle, but at that time known by another name).<ref name="DH1" /><ref name="WHG1">{{cite web |title=七草大祭だるま市｜2022年1月6-7日にだるま発祥の地少林山達磨寺で開催【高崎市鼻高町】 |url=https://we-love.gunma.jp/area/daruma |website=群馬の観光・旅行情報サイト We love群馬 |access-date=6 June 2022 |language=ja}}</ref>
== History == Originally Shorinzan was founded as a temple for the [[Sōtō|Soto]] school of Zen Buddhism, but in Meiji era was converted to the Obaku sect.
From 1783 to 1787, the 9th generation Zen Master of the temple aided suffering locals during the Great Tenmei famine. He taught them how to make papier-mache dolls based on daruma dolls. This began what is now known as the Shorinzan Nanakusa Taisai Daruma Market Festival. It is held anually on January 6th and 7th. In recent years it has attracted thousands of visitors each year who come to buy new daruma dolls and have their old ones burned.<ref name="WHG1" /><ref>{{cite web |title=Shorinzan Nanakusa Taisai Daruma Market Festival {{!}} Gunma Official Tourist Guide |url=https://www.visit-gunma.jp/en/spots/shorinzan-nanakusa-taisai-daruma-market-festival/ |website=visit-gunma.jp |publisher=Gunma Prefectual Government |access-date=6 June 2022}}</ref>
On June 14th 1881 Shorinzan burned down in a fire, losing important historical documents such as the details of its construction. There used to be a bell that was donated during World War II that was said to have an inscription inside dating it to 1712.
In 1934 German architetct [[Bruno Taut]] fled from Nazi Germany with his wife. He was invited to Japan and eventually to Takasaki. There was a small empty building available on the outskirts of Shorinzan where they lived for the two years they spent in Takasaki. At his farewell party held on October 10th 1936, Taut expressed his desire to have his bones buried at the temple after his death.<ref name="DH1" /><ref name="HP1">{{cite web |title=洗心亭 |url=http://www.city.takasaki.gunma.jp/docs/2013121700122/ |website=www.city.takasaki.gunma.jp |publisher=高崎市公式HP |access-date=6 June 2022}}</ref>
While still working as an assistant director, [[Akira Kurosawa]] wrote a script called ''[[List of works by Akira Kurosawa|A German at Daruma Temple]]'' that used Shorinzan as the setting. It was written sometime between the 1930's to 1940's but was never filmed.
In 1947, [[Jomo Karuta]] was created; it features Shorinzan as the 'e' card.
== Cultural Properties == Shorinzan is connected with two [[Cultural Property (Japan)|designated cultural properties]].<ref name="HP1" />
On the prefectual level, Senshin-tei (洗心亭). It was built in Taisho era and the size of 6x4 tatami mats. This is the building where Bruno Taut lived during his stay in Takasaki.
On the municipal level, the Daruma Daishi (Bodhidharma) statue (達磨大師立像).
==References== {{reflist}}

Shorinzan Daruma Temple (少林山達磨寺, shōrinzan daruma-ji) is a small temple located in [[Takasaki]], [[Gunma Prefecture]]. It was built in 1697 by the [[Obaku (school of Buddhism)|Obaku]] school of Zen Buddhism. Takasaki is known as the birthplace of [[Daruma doll|daruma]], as it is believed that the dolls originated from the region many years ago. The daruma of Shorinzan are said to be especially lucky, leading the city's PR department to dub Takasaki as The Lucky Town.<ref>{{cite web |title=The Lucky Town TAKASAKI |url=http://excitingcity-takasaki.com/ltt/index.html |website=excitingcity-takasaki.com |publisher=高崎市公式HP |access-date=6 June 2022}}</ref>
== Origin == An old legend states that sometime between 1673 and 1681, a heavy rain came and flooded the Usui River. Afterwards, villagers found a glowing, scented tree near the riverbed. They decided it was sacred and placed it inside an old hut that housed the local Kannon statue. As they did, purple haze began swirling around them.<ref name="DH1">{{cite web |title=【公式】黄檗宗 少林山達磨寺 |url=https://www.daruma.or.jp/ |website=www.daruma.or.jp |access-date=6 June 2022 |language=ja}}</ref>
Around the time of the flood, Bodhidharma Daishi appeared before a traveling monk named Ichiryo Koji in a dream. Alledgedly he commanded Ichiryo Koji to carve his likeness out of a tree that was exactly the height of his nose. In 1680 Ichiryo Koji began his search for that tree, finally finding it when he visited the village that had once flooded. Ichiryo Koji carved the Bodhidharma Daishi's likeness with great devotion and care; it was officially placed next to the Kannon statue, both enshrined together.<ref name="DH1" />
Ichiryo Koji's devotion inspired the local villagers, and after that the area became known as "Shorinzan", the sacred place where the Daruma appeared. The ruling lord of the time then had Shorinzan Daruma Temple founded in 1697 in order to protect his castle (historically, Maebashi Castle, but at that time known by another name).<ref name="DH1" /><ref name="WHG1">{{cite web |title=七草大祭だるま市｜2022年1月6-7日にだるま発祥の地少林山達磨寺で開催【高崎市鼻高町】 |url=https://we-love.gunma.jp/area/daruma |website=群馬の観光・旅行情報サイト We love群馬 |access-date=6 June 2022 |language=ja}}</ref>
== History == Originally Shorinzan was founded as a temple for the [[Sōtō|Soto]] school of Zen Buddhism, but in Meiji era was converted to the Obaku sect.
From 1783 to 1787, the 9th generation Zen Master of the temple aided suffering locals during the Great Tenmei famine. He taught them how to make papier-mache dolls based on daruma dolls. This began what is now known as the Shorinzan Nanakusa Taisai Daruma Market Festival. It is held anually on January 6th and 7th. In recent years it has attracted thousands of visitors each year who come to buy new daruma dolls and have their old ones burned.<ref name="WHG1" /><ref>{{cite web |title=Shorinzan Nanakusa Taisai Daruma Market Festival {{!}} Gunma Official Tourist Guide |url=https://www.visit-gunma.jp/en/spots/shorinzan-nanakusa-taisai-daruma-market-festival/ |website=visit-gunma.jp |publisher=Gunma Prefectual Government |access-date=6 June 2022}}</ref>
On June 14th 1881 Shorinzan burned down in a fire, losing important historical documents such as the details of its construction. There used to be a bell that was donated during World War II that was said to have an inscription inside dating it to 1712.
In 1934 German architetct [[Bruno Taut]] fled from Nazi Germany with his wife. He was invited to Japan and eventually to Takasaki. There was a small empty building available on the outskirts of Shorinzan where they lived for the two years they spent in Takasaki. At his farewell party held on October 10th 1936, Taut expressed his desire to have his bones buried at the temple after his death.<ref name="DH1" /><ref name="HP1">{{cite web |title=洗心亭 |url=http://www.city.takasaki.gunma.jp/docs/2013121700122/ |website=www.city.takasaki.gunma.jp |publisher=高崎市公式HP |access-date=6 June 2022}}</ref>
While still working as an assistant director, [[Akira Kurosawa]] wrote a script called ''[[List of works by Akira Kurosawa|A German at Daruma Temple]]'' that used Shorinzan as the setting. It was written sometime between the 1930's to 1940's but was never filmed.
In 1947, [[Jomo Karuta]] was created; it features Shorinzan as the 'e' card.
== Cultural Properties == Shorinzan is connected with two [[Cultural Property (Japan)|designated cultural properties]].<ref name="HP1" />
On the prefectual level, Senshin-tei (洗心亭). It was built in Taisho era and the size of 6x4 tatami mats. This is the building where Bruno Taut lived during his stay in Takasaki.
On the municipal level, the Daruma Daishi (Bodhidharma) statue (達磨大師立像).

The ruling lord of the time then had Shorinzan Daruma Temple founded in 1697 in order to protect his castle (historically, Maebashi Castle, but at that time known by another name).<ref name="DH1" /><ref name="WHG1">{{cite web |title=七草大祭だるま市｜2022年1月6-7日にだるま発祥の地少林山達磨寺で開催【高崎市鼻高町】 |url=https://we-love.gunma.jp/area/daruma |website=群馬の観光・旅行情報サイト We love群馬 |access-date=6 June 2022 |language=ja}}</ref>
== History == Originally Shorinzan was founded as a temple for the [[Sōtō|Soto]] school of Zen Buddhism, but in Meiji era was converted to the Obaku sect.
From 1783 to 1787, the 9th generation Zen Master of the temple aided suffering locals during the Great Tenmei famine. He taught them how to make papier-mache dolls based on daruma dolls. This began what is now known as the Shorinzan Nanakusa Taisai Daruma Market Festival. It is held anually on January 6 and 7. In recent years it has attracted thousands of visitors each year who come to buy new daruma dolls and have their old ones burned.<ref name="WHG1" /><ref>{{cite web |title=Shorinzan Nanakusa Taisai Daruma Market Festival {{!}} Gunma Official Tourist Guide |url=https://www.visit-gunma.jp/en/spots/shorinzan-nanakusa-taisai-daruma-market-festival/ |website=visit-gunma.jp |publisher=Gunma Prefectual Government |access-date=6 June 2022}}</ref>
On June 14, 1881 Shorinzan burned down in a fire, losing important historical documents such as the details of its construction. There used to be a bell that was donated during World War II that was said to have an inscription inside dating it to 1712.
In 1934 German architetct [[Bruno Taut]] fled from Nazi Germany with his wife. He was invited to Japan and eventually to Takasaki. There was a small empty building available on the outskirts of Shorinzan where they lived for the two years they spent in Takasaki. At his farewell party held on October 10, 1936, Taut expressed his desire to have his bones buried at the temple after his death.<ref name="DH1" /><ref name="HP1">{{cite web |title=洗心亭 |url=http://www.city.takasaki.gunma.jp/docs/2013121700122/ |website=www.city.takasaki.gunma.jp |publisher=高崎市公式HP |access-date=6 June 2022}}</ref>
While still working as an assistant director, [[Akira Kurosawa]] wrote a script called ''[[List of works by Akira Kurosawa|A German at Daruma Temple]]'' that used Shorinzan as the setting. It was written sometime between the 1930's to 1940's but was never filmed.
In 1947, [[Jomo Karuta]] was created; it features Shorinzan as the 'e' card.
== Cultural Properties == Shorinzan is connected with two [[Cultural Property (Japan)|designated cultural properties]].<ref name="HP1" />
On the prefectual level, Senshin-tei (洗心亭). It was built in Taisho era and the size of 6x4 tatami mats. This is the building where Bruno Taut lived during his stay in Takasaki.
On the municipal level, the Daruma Daishi (Bodhidharma) statue (達磨大師立像).

'''Shorinzan Daruma Temple''' (少林山達磨寺, shōrinzan daruma-ji) is a small temple located in [[Takasaki]], [[Gunma Prefecture]]. It was built in 1697 by the [[Obaku (school of Buddhism)|Obaku]] school of Zen Buddhism. Takasaki is known as the birthplace of [[Daruma doll|daruma]], as it is believed that the dolls originated from the region many years ago. The daruma of Shorinzan are said to be especially lucky, leading the city's PR department to dub Takasaki as The Lucky Town.<ref>{{cite web |title=The Lucky Town TAKASAKI |url=http://excitingcity-takasaki.com/ltt/index.html |website=excitingcity-takasaki.com |publisher=高崎市公式HP |access-date=6 June 2022}}</ref>
== Origin == An old legend states that sometime between 1673 and 1681, a heavy rain came and flooded the Usui River. Afterwards, villagers found a glowing, scented tree near the riverbed. They decided it was sacred and placed it inside an old hut that housed the local Kannon statue. As they did, purple haze began swirling around them.<ref name="DH1">{{cite web |title=【公式】黄檗宗 少林山達磨寺 |url=https://www.daruma.or.jp/ |website=www.daruma.or.jp |access-date=6 June 2022 |language=ja}}</ref>
Around the time of the flood, Bodhidharma Daishi appeared before a traveling monk named Ichiryo Koji in a dream. Alledgedly he commanded Ichiryo Koji to carve his likeness out of a tree that was exactly the height of his nose. In 1680 Ichiryo Koji began his search for that tree, finally finding it when he visited the village that had once flooded. Ichiryo Koji carved the Bodhidharma Daishi's likeness with great devotion and care; it was officially placed next to the Kannon statue, both enshrined together.<ref name="DH1" />
Ichiryo Koji's devotion inspired the local villagers, and after that the area became known as "Shorinzan", the sacred place where the Daruma appeared. The ruling lord of the time then had Shorinzan Daruma Temple founded in 1697 in order to protect his castle (historically, Maebashi Castle, but at that time known by another name).<ref name="DH1" /><ref name="WHG1">{{cite web |title=七草大祭だるま市｜2022年1月6-7日にだるま発祥の地少林山達磨寺で開催【高崎市鼻高町】 |url=https://we-love.gunma.jp/area/daruma |website=群馬の観光・旅行情報サイト We love群馬 |access-date=6 June 2022 |language=ja}}</ref>
== History == Originally Shorinzan was founded as a temple for the [[Sōtō|Soto]] school of Zen Buddhism, but in Meiji era was converted to the Obaku sect.
From 1783 to 1787, the 9th generation Zen Master of the temple aided suffering locals during the Great Tenmei famine. He taught them how to make papier-mache dolls based on daruma dolls. This began what is now known as the Shorinzan Nanakusa Taisai Daruma Market Festival. It is held anually on January 6 and 7. In recent years it has attracted thousands of visitors each year who come to buy new daruma dolls and have their old ones burned.<ref name="WHG1" /><ref>{{cite web |title=Shorinzan Nanakusa Taisai Daruma Market Festival {{!}} Gunma Official Tourist Guide |url=https://www.visit-gunma.jp/en/spots/shorinzan-nanakusa-taisai-daruma-market-festival/ |website=visit-gunma.jp |publisher=Gunma Prefectual Government |access-date=6 June 2022}}</ref>
On June 14, 1881 Shorinzan burned down in a fire, losing important historical documents such as the details of its construction. There used to be a bell that was donated during World War II that was said to have an inscription inside dating it to 1712.
In 1934 German architetct [[Bruno Taut]] fled from Nazi Germany with his wife. He was invited to Japan and eventually to Takasaki. There was a small empty building available on the outskirts of Shorinzan where they lived for the two years they spent in Takasaki. At his farewell party held on October 10, 1936, Taut expressed his desire to have his bones buried at the temple after his death.<ref name="DH1" /><ref name="HP1">{{cite web |title=洗心亭 |url=http://www.city.takasaki.gunma.jp/docs/2013121700122/ |website=www.city.takasaki.gunma.jp |publisher=高崎市公式HP |access-date=6 June 2022}}</ref>
While still working as an assistant director, [[Akira Kurosawa]] wrote a script called ''[[List of works by Akira Kurosawa|A German at Daruma Temple]]'' that used Shorinzan as the setting. It was written sometime between the 1930's to 1940's but was never filmed.
In 1947, [[Jomo Karuta]] was created; it features Shorinzan as the 'e' card.
== Cultural properties == Shorinzan is connected with two [[Cultural Property (Japan)|designated cultural properties]].<ref name="HP1" />
On the prefectual level, Senshin-tei (洗心亭). It was built in Taisho era and the size of 6x4 tatami mats. This is the building where Bruno Taut lived during his stay in Takasaki.
On the municipal level, the Daruma Daishi (Bodhidharma) statue (達磨大師立像).
==References== {{reflist}}
{{coord|36.3303|138.9569|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title}}
[[Category:Religious buildings and structures in Gunma Prefecture]]

In recent years it has attracted thousands of visitors each year who come to buy new daruma dolls and have their old ones burned.<ref name="WHG1" /><ref>{{cite web |title=Shorinzan Nanakusa Taisai Daruma Market Festival {{!}} Gunma Official Tourist Guide |url=https://www.visit-gunma.jp/en/spots/shorinzan-nanakusa-taisai-daruma-market-festival/ |website=visit-gunma.jp |publisher=Gunma Prefectural Government |access-date=6 June 2022}}</ref>
On June 14, 1881 Shorinzan burned down in a fire, losing important historical documents such as the details of its construction. There used to be a bell that was donated during World War II that was said to have an inscription inside dating it to 1712.
In 1934 German architect [[Bruno Taut]] fled from Nazi Germany with his wife. He was invited to Japan and eventually to Takasaki. There was a small empty building available on the outskirts of Shorinzan where they lived for the two years they spent in Takasaki. At his farewell party held on October 10, 1936, Taut expressed his desire to have his bones buried at the temple after his death.<ref name="DH1" /><ref name="HP1">{{cite web |title=洗心亭 |url=http://www.city.takasaki.gunma.jp/docs/2013121700122/ |website=www.city.takasaki.gunma.jp |publisher=高崎市公式HP |access-date=6 June 2022}}</ref>
While still working as an assistant director, [[Akira Kurosawa]] wrote a script called ''[[List of works by Akira Kurosawa|A German at Daruma Temple]]'' that used Shorinzan as the setting. It was written sometime between the 1930s to 1940s but was never filmed.
In 1947, [[Jomo Karuta]] was created; it features Shorinzan as the 'e' card.
== Cultural properties == Shorinzan is connected with two [[Cultural Property (Japan)|designated cultural properties]].<ref name="HP1" />
On the prefectural level, Senshin-tei (洗心亭). It was built in Taisho era and the size of 6x4 tatami mats. This is the building where Bruno Taut lived during his stay in Takasaki.
On the municipal level, the Daruma Daishi (Bodhidharma) statue (達磨大師立像).

{{Short description|Buddhist temple in Honshu, Japan}} '''Shorinzan Daruma Temple''' (少林山達磨寺, shōrinzan daruma-ji) is a small temple located in [[Takasaki]], [[Gunma Prefecture]]. It was built in 1697 by the [[Obaku (school of Buddhism)|Obaku]] school of Zen Buddhism. Takasaki is known as the birthplace of [[Daruma doll|daruma]], as it is believed that the dolls originated from the region many years ago. The daruma of Shorinzan are said to be especially lucky, leading the city's PR department to dub Takasaki as The Lucky Town.<ref>{{cite web |title=The Lucky Town TAKASAKI |url=http://excitingcity-takasaki.com/ltt/index.html |website=excitingcity-takasaki.com |publisher=高崎市公式HP |access-date=6 June 2022}}</ref>
== Origin == An old legend states that sometime between 1673 and 1681, a heavy rain came and flooded the Usui River. Afterwards, villagers found a glowing, scented tree near the riverbed. They decided it was sacred and placed it inside an old hut that housed the local Kannon statue. As they did, purple haze began swirling around them.<ref name="DH1">{{cite web |title=【公式】黄檗宗 少林山達磨寺 |url=https://www.daruma.or.jp/ |website=www.daruma.or.jp |access-date=6 June 2022 |language=ja}}</ref>
Around the time of the flood, Bodhidharma Daishi appeared before a traveling monk named Ichiryo Koji in a dream. Allegedly he commanded Ichiryo Koji to carve his likeness out of a tree that was exactly the height of his nose. In 1680 Ichiryo Koji began his search for that tree, finally finding it when he visited the village that had once flooded. Ichiryo Koji carved the Bodhidharma Daishi's likeness with great devotion and care; it was officially placed next to the Kannon statue, both enshrined together.<ref name="DH1" />
Ichiryo Koji's devotion inspired the local villagers, and after that the area became known as "Shorinzan", the sacred place where the Daruma appeared. The ruling lord of the time then had Shorinzan Daruma Temple founded in 1697 in order to protect his castle (historically, Maebashi Castle, but at that time known by another name).<ref name="DH1" /><ref name="WHG1">{{cite web |title=七草大祭だるま市｜2022年1月6-7日にだるま発祥の地少林山達磨寺で開催【高崎市鼻高町】 |url=https://we-love.gunma.jp/area/daruma |website=群馬の観光・旅行情報サイト We love群馬 |access-date=6 June 2022 |language=ja}}</ref>
== History == Originally Shorinzan was founded as a temple for the [[Sōtō|Soto]] school of Zen Buddhism, but in Meiji era was converted to the Obaku sect.
From 1783 to 1787, the 9th generation Zen Master of the temple aided suffering locals during the Great Tenmei famine. He taught them how to make papier-mâché dolls based on daruma dolls. This began what is now known as the Shorinzan Nanakusa Taisai Daruma Market Festival. It is held annually on January 6 and 7. In recent years it has attracted thousands of visitors each year who come to buy new daruma dolls and have their old ones burned.<ref name="WHG1" /><ref>{{cite web |title=Shorinzan Nanakusa Taisai Daruma Market Festival {{!}} Gunma Official Tourist Guide |url=https://www.visit-gunma.jp/en/spots/shorinzan-nanakusa-taisai-daruma-market-festival/ |website=visit-gunma.jp |publisher=Gunma Prefectural Government |access-date=6 June 2022}}</ref>
On June 14, 1881 Shorinzan burned down in a fire, losing important historical documents such as the details of its construction. There used to be a bell that was donated during World War II that was said to have an inscription inside dating it to 1712.
In 1934 German architect [[Bruno Taut]] fled from Nazi Germany with his wife. He was invited to Japan and eventually to Takasaki. There was a small empty building available on the outskirts of Shorinzan where they lived for the two years they spent in Takasaki. At his farewell party held on October 10, 1936, Taut expressed his desire to have his bones buried at the temple after his death.<ref name="DH1" /><ref name="HP1">{{cite web |title=洗心亭 |url=http://www.city.takasaki.gunma.jp/docs/2013121700122/ |website=www.city.takasaki.gunma.jp |publisher=高崎市公式HP |access-date=6 June 2022}}</ref>
While still working as an assistant director, [[Akira Kurosawa]] wrote a script called ''[[List of works by Akira Kurosawa|A German at Daruma Temple]]'' that used Shorinzan as the setting. It was written sometime between the 1930s to 1940s but was never filmed.
In 1947, [[Jomo Karuta]] was created; it features Shorinzan as the 'e' card.
== Cultural properties == Shorinzan is connected with two [[Cultural Property (Japan)|designated cultural properties]].<ref name="HP1" />
On the prefectural level, Senshin-tei (洗心亭). It was built in Taisho era and the size of 6x4 tatami mats. This is the building where Bruno Taut lived during his stay in Takasaki.
On the municipal level, the Daruma Daishi (Bodhidharma) statue (達磨大師立像).

In recent years it has attracted thousands of visitors each year who come to buy new daruma dolls and have their old ones burned.<ref name="WHG1" /><ref>{{cite web |title=Shorinzan Nanakusa Taisai Daruma Market Festival {{!}} Gunma Official Tourist Guide |url=https://www.visit-gunma.jp/en/spots/shorinzan-nanakusa-taisai-daruma-market-festival/ |website=visit-gunma.jp |publisher=Gunma Prefectural Government |access-date=6 June 2022}}</ref>
On June 14, 1881 Shorinzan burned down in a fire, losing important historical documents such as the details of its construction. There used to be a bell that was donated during World War II that was said to have an inscription inside dating it to 1712.
In 1934 German architect [[Bruno Taut]] fled from Nazi Germany with his wife. He was invited to Japan and eventually to Takasaki. There was a small empty building available on the outskirts of Shorinzan where they lived for the two years they spent in Takasaki. At his farewell party held on October 10, 1936, Taut expressed his desire to have his bones buried at the temple after his death.<ref name="DH1" /><ref name="HP1">{{cite web |title=洗心亭 |url=http://www.city.takasaki.gunma.jp/docs/2013121700122/ |website=www.city.takasaki.gunma.jp |publisher=高崎市公式HP |access-date=6 June 2022}}</ref>
While still working as an assistant director, [[Akira Kurosawa]] wrote a script called ''[[List of works by Akira Kurosawa|A German at Daruma Temple]]'' that used Shorinzan as the setting. It was written sometime between the 1930s to 1940s but was never filmed.
In 1947, [[Jomo Karuta]] was created; it features Shorinzan as the 'e' card.
== Cultural properties == Shorinzan is connected with two [[Cultural Property (Japan)|designated cultural properties]].<ref name="HP1" />
On the prefectural level, Senshin-tei (洗心亭). It was built in Taisho era and the size of 6x4 tatami mats. This is the building where Bruno Taut lived during his stay in Takasaki.
On the municipal level, the Daruma Daishi (Bodhidharma) statue (達磨大師立像).

{{Short description|Buddhist temple in Honshu, Japan}} '''Shorinzan Daruma Temple''' (少林山達磨寺, shōrinzan daruma-ji) is a small temple located in [[Takasaki]], [[Gunma Prefecture]]. It was built in 1697 by the [[Obaku (school of Buddhism)|Obaku]] school of Zen Buddhism. Takasaki is known as the birthplace of [[Daruma doll|daruma]], as it is believed that the dolls originated from the region many years ago. The daruma of Shorinzan are said to be especially lucky, leading the city's PR department to dub Takasaki as The Lucky Town.<ref>{{cite web |title=The Lucky Town TAKASAKI |url=http://excitingcity-takasaki.com/ltt/index.html |website=excitingcity-takasaki.com |publisher=高崎市公式HP |access-date=6 June 2022}}</ref>
== Origin == An old legend states that sometime between 1673 and 1681, a heavy rain came and flooded the Usui River. Afterwards, villagers found a glowing, scented tree near the riverbed. They decided it was sacred and placed it inside an old hut that housed the local Kannon statue. As they did, purple haze began swirling around them.<ref name="DH1">{{cite web |title=【公式】黄檗宗 少林山達磨寺 |url=https://www.daruma.or.jp/ |website=www.daruma.or.jp |access-date=6 June 2022 |language=ja}}</ref>
Around the time of the flood, Bodhidharma Daishi appeared before a traveling monk named Ichiryo Koji in a dream. Allegedly he commanded Ichiryo Koji to carve his likeness out of a tree that was exactly the height of his nose. In 1680 Ichiryo Koji began his search for that tree, finally finding it when he visited the village that had once flooded. Ichiryo Koji carved the Bodhidharma Daishi's likeness with great devotion and care; it was officially placed next to the Kannon statue, both enshrined together.<ref name="DH1" />
Ichiryo Koji's devotion inspired the local villagers, and after that the area became known as "Shorinzan", the sacred place where the Daruma appeared. The ruling lord of the time then had Shorinzan Daruma Temple founded in 1697 in order to protect his castle (historically, Maebashi Castle, but at that time known by another name).<ref name="DH1" /><ref name="WHG1">{{cite web |title=七草大祭だるま市｜2022年1月6-7日にだるま発祥の地少林山達磨寺で開催【高崎市鼻高町】 |url=https://we-love.gunma.jp/area/daruma |website=群馬の観光・旅行情報サイト We love群馬 |access-date=6 June 2022 |language=ja}}</ref>
== History == Originally Shorinzan was founded as a temple for the [[Sōtō|Soto]] school of Zen Buddhism, but in Meiji era was converted to the Obaku sect.
From 1783 to 1787, the 9th generation Zen Master of the temple aided suffering locals during the Great Tenmei famine. He taught them how to make papier-mâché dolls based on daruma dolls. This began what is now known as the Shorinzan Nanakusa Taisai Daruma Market Festival. It is held annually on January 6 and 7. In recent years it has attracted thousands of visitors each year who come to buy new daruma dolls and have their old ones burned.<ref name="WHG1" /><ref>{{cite web |title=Shorinzan Nanakusa Taisai Daruma Market Festival {{!}} Gunma Official Tourist Guide |url=https://www.visit-gunma.jp/en/spots/shorinzan-nanakusa-taisai-daruma-market-festival/ |website=visit-gunma.jp |publisher=Gunma Prefectural Government |access-date=6 June 2022}}</ref>
On June 14, 1881 Shorinzan burned down in a fire, losing important historical documents such as the details of its construction. There used to be a bell that was donated during World War II that was said to have an inscription inside dating it to 1712.
In 1934 German architect [[Bruno Taut]] fled from Nazi Germany with his wife. He was invited to Japan and eventually to Takasaki. There was a small empty building available on the outskirts of Shorinzan where they lived for the two years they spent in Takasaki. At his farewell party held on October 10, 1936, Taut expressed his desire to have his bones buried at the temple after his death.<ref name="DH1" /><ref name="HP1">{{cite web |title=洗心亭 |url=http://www.city.takasaki.gunma.jp/docs/2013121700122/ |website=www.city.takasaki.gunma.jp |publisher=高崎市公式HP |access-date=6 June 2022}}</ref>
While still working as an assistant director, [[Akira Kurosawa]] wrote a script called ''[[List of works by Akira Kurosawa|A German at Daruma Temple]]'' that used Shorinzan as the setting. It was written sometime between the 1930s to 1940s but was never filmed.
In 1947, [[Jomo Karuta]] was created; it features Shorinzan as the 'e' card.
== Cultural properties == Shorinzan is connected with two [[Cultural Property (Japan)|designated cultural properties]].<ref name="HP1" />
On the prefectural level, Senshin-tei (洗心亭). It was built in Taisho era and the size of 6x4 tatami mats. This is the building where Bruno Taut lived during his stay in Takasaki.
On the municipal level, the Daruma Daishi (Bodhidharma) statue (達磨大師立像).

In recent years it has attracted thousands of visitors each year who come to buy new daruma dolls and have their old ones burned.<ref name="WHG1" /><ref>{{cite web |title=Shorinzan Nanakusa Taisai Daruma Market Festival {{!}} Gunma Official Tourist Guide |url=https://www.visit-gunma.jp/en/spots/shorinzan-nanakusa-taisai-daruma-market-festival/ |website=visit-gunma.jp |publisher=Gunma Prefectural Government |access-date=6 June 2022}}</ref>
On June 14, 1881 Shorinzan burned down in a fire, losing important historical documents such as the details of its construction. There used to be a bell that was donated during World War II that was said to have an inscription inside dating it to 1712.
In 1934 German architect [[Bruno Taut]] fled from Nazi Germany with his wife. He was invited to Japan and eventually to Takasaki. There was a small empty building available on the outskirts of Shorinzan where they lived for the two years they spent in Takasaki. At his farewell party held on October 10, 1936, Taut expressed his desire to have his bones buried at the temple after his death.<ref name="DH1" /><ref name="HP1">{{cite web |title=洗心亭 |url=http://www.city.takasaki.gunma.jp/docs/2013121700122/ |website=www.city.takasaki.gunma.jp |publisher=高崎市公式HP |access-date=6 June 2022}}</ref>
While still working as an assistant director, [[Akira Kurosawa]] wrote a script called ''[[List of works by Akira Kurosawa|A German at Daruma Temple]]'' that used Shorinzan as the setting. It was written sometime between the 1930s to 1940s but was never filmed.
In 1947, [[Jomo Karuta]] was created; it features Shorinzan as the 'e' card.
== Cultural properties == Shorinzan is connected with two [[Cultural Property (Japan)|designated cultural properties]].<ref name="HP1" />
On the prefectural level, Senshin-tei (洗心亭). It was built in Taisho era and the size of 6x4 tatami mats. This is the building where Bruno Taut lived during his stay in Takasaki.
On the municipal level, the Daruma Daishi (Bodhidharma) statue (達磨大師立像).

{{Short description|Buddhist temple in Honshu, Japan}} '''Shorinzan Daruma Temple''' (少林山達磨寺, shōrinzan daruma-ji) is a small temple located in [[Takasaki]], [[Gunma Prefecture]]. It was built in 1697 by the [[Obaku (school of Buddhism)|Obaku]] school of Zen Buddhism. Takasaki is known as the birthplace of [[Daruma doll|daruma]], as it is believed that the dolls originated from the region many years ago. The daruma of Shorinzan are said to be especially lucky, leading the city's PR department to dub Takasaki as The Lucky Town.<ref>{{cite web |title=The Lucky Town TAKASAKI |url=http://excitingcity-takasaki.com/ltt/index.html |website=excitingcity-takasaki.com |publisher=高崎市公式HP |access-date=6 June 2022}}</ref>
== Origin == An old legend states that sometime between 1673 and 1681, a heavy rain came and flooded the Usui River. Afterwards, villagers found a glowing, scented tree near the riverbed. They decided it was sacred and placed it inside an old hut that housed the local Kannon statue. As they did, purple haze began swirling around them.<ref name="DH1">{{cite web |title=【公式】黄檗宗 少林山達磨寺 |url=https://www.daruma.or.jp/ |website=www.daruma.or.jp |access-date=6 June 2022 |language=ja}}</ref>
Around the time of the flood, Bodhidharma Daishi appeared before a traveling monk named Ichiryo Koji in a dream. Allegedly he commanded Ichiryo Koji to carve his likeness out of a tree that was exactly the height of his nose. In 1680 Ichiryo Koji began his search for that tree, finally finding it when he visited the village that had once flooded. Ichiryo Koji carved the Bodhidharma Daishi's likeness with great devotion and care; it was officially placed next to the Kannon statue, both enshrined together.<ref name="DH1" />
Ichiryo Koji's devotion inspired the local villagers, and after that the area became known as "Shorinzan", the sacred place where the Daruma appeared. The ruling lord of the time then had Shorinzan Daruma Temple founded in 1697 in order to protect his castle (historically, Maebashi Castle, but at that time known by another name).<ref name="DH1" /><ref name="WHG1">{{cite web |title=七草大祭だるま市｜2022年1月6-7日にだるま発祥の地少林山達磨寺で開催【高崎市鼻高町】 |url=https://we-love.gunma.jp/area/daruma |website=群馬の観光・旅行情報サイト We love群馬 |access-date=6 June 2022 |language=ja}}</ref>
== History == Originally Shorinzan was founded as a temple for the [[Sōtō|Soto]] school of Zen Buddhism, but in Meiji era was converted to the Obaku sect.
From 1783 to 1787, the 9th generation Zen Master of the temple aided suffering locals during the Great Tenmei famine. He taught them how to make papier-mâché dolls based on daruma dolls. This began what is now known as the Shorinzan Nanakusa Taisai Daruma Market Festival. It is held annually on January 6 and 7. In recent years it has attracted thousands of visitors each year who come to buy new daruma dolls and have their old ones burned.<ref name="WHG1" /><ref>{{cite web |title=Shorinzan Nanakusa Taisai Daruma Market Festival {{!}} Gunma Official Tourist Guide |url=https://www.visit-gunma.jp/en/spots/shorinzan-nanakusa-taisai-daruma-market-festival/ |website=visit-gunma.jp |publisher=Gunma Prefectural Government |access-date=6 June 2022}}</ref>
On June 14, 1881 Shorinzan burned down in a fire, losing important historical documents such as the details of its construction. There used to be a bell that was donated during World War II that was said to have an inscription inside dating it to 1712.
In 1934 German architect [[Bruno Taut]] fled from Nazi Germany with his wife. He was invited to Japan and eventually to Takasaki. There was a small empty building available on the outskirts of Shorinzan where they lived for the two years they spent in Takasaki. At his farewell party held on October 10, 1936, Taut expressed his desire to have his bones buried at the temple after his death.<ref name="DH1" /><ref name="HP1">{{cite web |title=洗心亭 |url=http://www.city.takasaki.gunma.jp/docs/2013121700122/ |website=www.city.takasaki.gunma.jp |publisher=高崎市公式HP |access-date=6 June 2022}}</ref>
While still working as an assistant director, [[Akira Kurosawa]] wrote a script called ''[[List of works by Akira Kurosawa|A German at Daruma Temple]]'' that used Shorinzan as the setting. It was written sometime between the 1930s to 1940s but was never filmed.
In 1947, [[Jomo Karuta]] was created; it features Shorinzan as the 'e' card.
== Cultural properties == Shorinzan is connected with two [[Cultural Property (Japan)|designated cultural properties]].<ref name="HP1" />
On the prefectural level, Senshin-tei (洗心亭). It was built in Taisho era and the size of 6x4 tatami mats. This is the building where Bruno Taut lived during his stay in Takasaki.
On the municipal level, the Daruma Daishi (Bodhidharma) statue (達磨大師立像).

From 1783 to 1787, the 9th generation Zen Master of the temple aided suffering locals during the Great Tenmei famine. He taught them how to make papier-mâché dolls based on daruma dolls. This began what is now known as the Shorinzan Nanakusa Taisai Daruma Market Festival. It is held annually on January 6 and 7. In recent years it has attracted thousands of visitors each year who come to buy new daruma dolls and have their old ones burned.<ref name="WHG1" /><ref>{{cite web |title=Shorinzan Nanakusa Taisai Daruma Market Festival {{!}} Gunma Official Tourist Guide |url=https://www.visit-gunma.jp/en/spots/shorinzan-nanakusa-taisai-daruma-market-festival/ |website=visit-gunma.jp |publisher=Gunma Prefectural Government |access-date=6 June 2022}}</ref>
On June 14, 1881 Shorinzan burned down in a fire, losing important historical documents such as the details of its construction. There used to be a bell that was donated during World War II that was said to have an inscription inside dating it to 1712.
In 1934 German architect [[Bruno Taut]] fled from Nazi Germany with his wife. He was invited to Japan and eventually to Takasaki. There was a small empty building available on the outskirts of Shorinzan where they lived for the two years they spent in Takasaki. At his farewell party held on October 10, 1936, Taut expressed his desire to have his bones buried at the temple after his death.<ref name="DH1" /><ref name="HP1">{{cite web |title=洗心亭 |url=http://www.city.takasaki.gunma.jp/docs/2013121700122/ |website=www.city.takasaki.gunma.jp |publisher=高崎市公式HP |access-date=6 June 2022}}</ref>
While still working as an assistant director, [[Akira Kurosawa]] wrote a script called ''[[List of works by Akira Kurosawa|A German at Daruma Temple]]'' that used Shorinzan as the setting. It was written sometime between the 1930s to 1940s but was never filmed.
In 1947, [[Jomo Karuta]] was created; it features Shorinzan as the 'e' card.
== Cultural properties == Shorinzan is connected with two [[Cultural Property (Japan)|designated cultural properties]].<ref name="HP1" />
On the prefectural level, Senshin-tei (洗心亭). It was built in Taisho era and the size of 6x4 tatami mats. This is the building where Bruno Taut lived during his stay in Takasaki.
On the municipal level, the Daruma Daishi (Bodhidharma) statue (達磨大師立像).

{{Short description|Buddhist temple in Honshu, Japan}} '''Shorinzan Daruma Temple''' (少林山達磨寺, shōrinzan daruma-ji) is a small temple located in [[Takasaki]], [[Gunma Prefecture]]. It was built in 1697 by the [[Obaku (school of Buddhism)|Obaku]] school of Zen Buddhism. Takasaki is known as the birthplace of [[Daruma doll|daruma]], as it is believed that the dolls originated from the region many years ago. The daruma of Shorinzan are said to be especially lucky, leading the city's PR department to dub Takasaki as The Lucky Town.<ref>{{cite web |title=The Lucky Town TAKASAKI |url=http://excitingcity-takasaki.com/ltt/index.html |website=excitingcity-takasaki.com |publisher=高崎市公式HP |access-date=6 June 2022}}</ref>
== Origin == An old legend states that sometime between 1673 and 1681, a heavy rain came and flooded the Usui River. Afterwards, villagers found a glowing, scented tree near the riverbed. They decided it was sacred and placed it inside an old hut that housed the local Kannon statue. As they did, purple haze began swirling around them.<ref name="DH1">{{cite web |title=【公式】黄檗宗 少林山達磨寺 |url=https://www.daruma.or.jp/ |website=www.daruma.or.jp |access-date=6 June 2022 |language=ja}}</ref>
Around the time of the flood, Bodhidharma Daishi appeared before a traveling monk named Ichiryo Koji in a dream. Allegedly he commanded Ichiryo Koji to carve his likeness out of a tree that was exactly the height of his nose. In 1680 Ichiryo Koji began his search for that tree, finally finding it when he visited the village that had once flooded. Ichiryo Koji carved the Bodhidharma Daishi's likeness with great devotion and care; it was officially placed next to the Kannon statue, both enshrined together.<ref name="DH1" />
Ichiryo Koji's devotion inspired the local villagers, and after that the area became known as "Shorinzan", the sacred place where the Daruma appeared. The ruling lord of the time then had Shorinzan Daruma Temple founded in 1697 in order to protect his castle (historically, Maebashi Castle, but at that time known by another name).<ref name="DH1" /><ref name="WHG1">{{cite web |title=七草大祭だるま市｜2022年1月6-7日にだるま発祥の地少林山達磨寺で開催【高崎市鼻高町】 |url=https://we-love.gunma.jp/area/daruma |website=群馬の観光・旅行情報サイト We love群馬 |access-date=6 June 2022 |language=ja}}</ref>
== History == Originally Shorinzan was founded as a temple for the [[Sōtō|Soto]] school of Zen Buddhism, but in Meiji era was converted to the Obaku sect.
From 1783 to 1787, the 9th generation Zen Master of the temple aided suffering locals during the Great Tenmei famine. He taught them how to make papier-mâché dolls based on daruma dolls. This began what is now known as the Shorinzan Nanakusa Taisai Daruma Market Festival. It is held annually on January 6 and 7. In recent years it has attracted thousands of visitors each year who come to buy new daruma dolls and have their old ones burned.<ref name="WHG1" /><ref>{{cite web |title=Shorinzan Nanakusa Taisai Daruma Market Festival {{!}} Gunma Official Tourist Guide |url=https://www.visit-gunma.jp/en/spots/shorinzan-nanakusa-taisai-daruma-market-festival/ |website=visit-gunma.jp |publisher=Gunma Prefectural Government |access-date=6 June 2022}}</ref>
On June 14, 1881 Shorinzan burned down in a fire, losing important historical documents such as the details of its construction. There used to be a bell that was donated during World War II that was said to have an inscription inside dating it to 1712.
In 1934 German architect [[Bruno Taut]] fled from Nazi Germany with his wife. He was invited to Japan and eventually to Takasaki. There was a small empty building available on the outskirts of Shorinzan where they lived for the two years they spent in Takasaki. At his farewell party held on October 10, 1936, Taut expressed his desire to have his bones buried at the temple after his death.<ref name="DH1" /><ref name="HP1">{{cite web |title=洗心亭 |url=http://www.city.takasaki.gunma.jp/docs/2013121700122/ |website=www.city.takasaki.gunma.jp |publisher=高崎市公式HP |access-date=6 June 2022}}</ref>
While still working as an assistant director, [[Akira Kurosawa]] wrote a script called ''[[List of works by Akira Kurosawa|A German at Daruma Temple]]'' that used Shorinzan as the setting. It was written sometime between the 1930s to 1940s but was never filmed.
In 1947, [[Jomo Karuta]] was created; it features Shorinzan as the 'e' card.
== Cultural properties == Shorinzan is connected with two [[Cultural Property (Japan)|designated cultural properties]].<ref name="HP1" />
On the prefectural level, Senshin-tei (洗心亭). It was built in Taisho era and the size of 6x4 tatami mats. This is the building where Bruno Taut lived during his stay in Takasaki.
On the municipal level, the Daruma Daishi (Bodhidharma) statue (達磨大師立像).

The ruling lord of the time then had Shorinzan Daruma Temple founded in 1697 in order to protect his castle (historically, Maebashi Castle, but at that time known by another name).<ref name="DH1" /><ref name="WHG1">{{cite web |title=七草大祭だるま市｜2022年1月6-7日にだるま発祥の地少林山達磨寺で開催【高崎市鼻高町】 |url=https://we-love.gunma.jp/area/daruma |website=群馬の観光・旅行情報サイト We love群馬 |access-date=6 June 2022 |language=ja}}</ref>
== History == Originally Shorinzan was founded as a temple for the [[Sōtō|Soto]] school of Zen Buddhism, but in Meiji era was converted to the Obaku sect.
From 1783 to 1787, the 9th generation Zen Master of the temple aided suffering locals during the Great Tenmei famine. He taught them how to make papier-mâché dolls based on daruma dolls. This began what is now known as the Shorinzan Nanakusa Taisai Daruma Market Festival. It is held annually on January 6 and 7. In recent years it has attracted thousands of visitors each year who come to buy new daruma dolls and have their old ones burned.<ref name="WHG1" /><ref>{{cite web |title=Shorinzan Nanakusa Taisai Daruma Market Festival {{!}} Gunma Official Tourist Guide |url=https://www.visit-gunma.jp/en/spots/shorinzan-nanakusa-taisai-daruma-market-festival/ |website=visit-gunma.jp |publisher=Gunma Prefectural Government |access-date=6 June 2022}}</ref>
On June 14, 1881 Shorinzan burned down in a fire, losing important historical documents such as the details of its construction. There used to be a bell that was donated during World War II that was said to have an inscription inside dating it to 1712.
In 1934 German architect [[Bruno Taut]] fled from Nazi Germany with his wife. He was invited to Japan and eventually to Takasaki. There was a small empty building available on the outskirts of Shorinzan where they lived for the two years they spent in Takasaki. At his farewell party held on October 10, 1936, Taut expressed his desire to have his bones buried at the temple after his death.<ref name="DH1" /><ref name="HP1">{{cite web |title=洗心亭 |url=http://www.city.takasaki.gunma.jp/docs/2013121700122/ |website=www.city.takasaki.gunma.jp |publisher=高崎市公式HP |access-date=6 June 2022}}</ref>
While still working as an assistant director, [[Akira Kurosawa]] wrote a script called ''[[List of works by Akira Kurosawa|A German at Daruma Temple]]'' that used Shorinzan as the setting. It was written sometime between the 1930s to 1940s but was never filmed.
In 1947, [[Jomo Karuta]] was created; it features Shorinzan as the 'e' card.
== Cultural properties == Shorinzan is connected with two [[Cultural Property (Japan)|designated cultural properties]].<ref name="HP1" />
On the prefectural level, Senshin-tei (洗心亭). It was built in Taisho era and the size of 6x4 tatami mats. This is the building where Bruno Taut lived during his stay in Takasaki.
On the municipal level, the Daruma Daishi (Bodhidharma) statue (達磨大師立像).

{{Short description|Buddhist temple in Honshu, Japan}} '''Shorinzan Daruma Temple''' (少林山達磨寺, shōrinzan daruma-ji) is a small temple located in [[Takasaki]], [[Gunma Prefecture]]. It was built in 1697 by the [[Obaku (school of Buddhism)|Obaku]] school of Zen Buddhism. Takasaki is known as the birthplace of [[Daruma doll|daruma]], as it is believed that the dolls originated from the region many years ago. The daruma of Shorinzan are said to be especially lucky, leading the city's PR department to dub Takasaki as The Lucky Town.<ref>{{cite web |title=The Lucky Town TAKASAKI |url=http://excitingcity-takasaki.com/ltt/index.html |website=excitingcity-takasaki.com |publisher=高崎市公式HP |access-date=6 June 2022}}</ref>
== Origin == An old legend states that sometime between 1673 and 1681, a heavy rain came and flooded the Usui River. Afterwards, villagers found a glowing, scented tree near the riverbed. They decided it was sacred and placed it inside an old hut that housed the local Kannon statue. As they did, purple haze began swirling around them.<ref name="DH1">{{cite web |title=【公式】黄檗宗 少林山達磨寺 |url=https://www.daruma.or.jp/ |website=www.daruma.or.jp |access-date=6 June 2022 |language=ja}}</ref>
Around the time of the flood, Bodhidharma Daishi appeared before a traveling monk named Ichiryo Koji in a dream. Allegedly he commanded Ichiryo Koji to carve his likeness out of a tree that was exactly the height of his nose. In 1680 Ichiryo Koji began his search for that tree, finally finding it when he visited the village that had once flooded. Ichiryo Koji carved the Bodhidharma Daishi's likeness with great devotion and care; it was officially placed next to the Kannon statue, both enshrined together.<ref name="DH1" />
Ichiryo Koji's devotion inspired the local villagers, and after that the area became known as "Shorinzan", the sacred place where the Daruma appeared. The ruling lord of the time then had Shorinzan Daruma Temple founded in 1697 in order to protect his castle (historically, Maebashi Castle, but at that time known by another name).<ref name="DH1" /><ref name="WHG1">{{cite web |title=七草大祭だるま市｜2022年1月6-7日にだるま発祥の地少林山達磨寺で開催【高崎市鼻高町】 |url=https://we-love.gunma.jp/area/daruma |website=群馬の観光・旅行情報サイト We love群馬 |access-date=6 June 2022 |language=ja}}</ref>
== History == Originally Shorinzan was founded as a temple for the [[Sōtō|Soto]] school of Zen Buddhism, but in Meiji era was converted to the Obaku sect.
From 1783 to 1787, the 9th generation Zen Master of the temple aided suffering locals during the Great Tenmei famine. He taught them how to make papier-mâché dolls based on daruma dolls. This began what is now known as the Shorinzan Nanakusa Taisai Daruma Market Festival. It is held annually on January 6 and 7. In recent years it has attracted thousands of visitors each year who come to buy new daruma dolls and have their old ones burned.<ref name="WHG1" /><ref>{{cite web |title=Shorinzan Nanakusa Taisai Daruma Market Festival {{!}} Gunma Official Tourist Guide |url=https://www.visit-gunma.jp/en/spots/shorinzan-nanakusa-taisai-daruma-market-festival/ |website=visit-gunma.jp |publisher=Gunma Prefectural Government |access-date=6 June 2022}}</ref>
On June 14, 1881 Shorinzan burned down in a fire, losing important historical documents such as the details of its construction. There used to be a bell that was donated during World War II that was said to have an inscription inside dating it to 1712.
In 1934 German architect [[Bruno Taut]] fled from Nazi Germany with his wife. He was invited to Japan and eventually to Takasaki. There was a small empty building available on the outskirts of Shorinzan where they lived for the two years they spent in Takasaki. At his farewell party held on October 10, 1936, Taut expressed his desire to have his bones buried at the temple after his death.<ref name="DH1" /><ref name="HP1">{{cite web |title=洗心亭 |url=http://www.city.takasaki.gunma.jp/docs/2013121700122/ |website=www.city.takasaki.gunma.jp |publisher=高崎市公式HP |access-date=6 June 2022}}</ref>
While still working as an assistant director, [[Akira Kurosawa]] wrote a script called ''[[List of works by Akira Kurosawa|A German at Daruma Temple]]'' that used Shorinzan as the setting. It was written sometime between the 1930s to 1940s but was never filmed.
In 1947, [[Jomo Karuta]] was created; it features Shorinzan as the 'e' card.

On the prefectural level, Senshin-tei (洗心亭). It was built in Taisho era and the size of 6x4 tatami mats. This is the building where Bruno Taut lived during his stay in Takasaki.
On the municipal level, the Daruma Daishi (Bodhidharma) statue (達磨大師立像).

It was built in 1697 by the [[Obaku (school of Buddhism)|Obaku]] school of Zen Buddhism. Takasaki is known as the birthplace of [[Daruma doll|daruma]], as it is believed that the dolls originated from the region many years ago. The daruma of Shorinzan are said to be especially lucky, leading the city's PR department to dub Takasaki as The Lucky Town.<ref>{{cite web |title=The Lucky Town TAKASAKI |url=http://excitingcity-takasaki.com/ltt/index.html |website=excitingcity-takasaki.com |publisher=高崎市公式HP |access-date=6 June 2022}}</ref>
== Origin == An old legend states that sometime between 1673 and 1681, a heavy rain came and flooded the Usui River. Afterwards, villagers found a glowing, scented tree near the riverbed. They decided it was sacred and placed it inside an old hut that housed the local Kannon statue. As they did, purple haze began swirling around them.<ref name="DH1">{{cite web |title=【公式】黄檗宗 少林山達磨寺 |url=https://www.daruma.or.jp/ |website=www.daruma.or.jp |access-date=6 June 2022 |language=ja}}</ref>
Around the time of the flood, Bodhidharma Daishi appeared before a traveling monk named Ichiryo Koji in a dream. Allegedly he commanded Ichiryo Koji to carve his likeness out of a tree that was exactly the height of his nose. In 1680 Ichiryo Koji began his search for that tree, finally finding it when he visited the village that had once flooded. Ichiryo Koji carved the Bodhidharma Daishi's likeness with great devotion and care; it was officially placed next to the Kannon statue, both enshrined together.<ref name="DH1" />
Ichiryo Koji's devotion inspired the local villagers, and after that the area became known as "Shorinzan", the sacred place where the Daruma appeared. The ruling lord of the time then had Shorinzan Daruma Temple founded in 1697 in order to protect his castle (historically, Maebashi Castle, but at that time known by another name).<ref name="DH1" /><ref name="WHG1">{{cite web |title=七草大祭だるま市｜2022年1月6-7日にだるま発祥の地少林山達磨寺で開催【高崎市鼻高町】 |url=https://we-love.gunma.jp/area/daruma |website=群馬の観光・旅行情報サイト We love群馬 |access-date=6 June 2022 |language=ja}}</ref>
== History == Originally Shorinzan was founded as a temple for the [[Sōtō|Soto]] school of Zen Buddhism, but in Meiji era was converted to the Obaku sect.
From 1783 to 1787, the 9th generation Zen Master of the temple aided suffering locals during the Great Tenmei famine. He taught them how to make papier-mâché dolls based on daruma dolls. This began what is now known as the Shorinzan Nanakusa Taisai Daruma Market Festival. It is held annually on January 6 and 7. In recent years it has attracted thousands of visitors each year who come to buy new daruma dolls and have their old ones burned.<ref name="WHG1" /><ref>{{cite web |title=Shorinzan Nanakusa Taisai Daruma Market Festival {{!}} Gunma Official Tourist Guide |url=https://www.visit-gunma.jp/en/spots/shorinzan-nanakusa-taisai-daruma-market-festival/ |website=visit-gunma.jp |publisher=Gunma Prefectural Government |access-date=6 June 2022}}</ref>
On June 14, 1881 Shorinzan burned down in a fire, losing important historical documents such as the details of its construction. There used to be a bell that was donated during World War II that was said to have an inscription inside dating it to 1712.
In 1934 German architect [[Bruno Taut]] fled from Nazi Germany with his wife. He was invited to Japan and eventually to Takasaki. There was a small empty building available on the outskirts of Shorinzan where they lived for the two years they spent in Takasaki. At his farewell party held on October 10, 1936, Taut expressed his desire to have his bones buried at the temple after his death.<ref name="DH1" /><ref name="HP1">{{cite web |title=洗心亭 |url=http://www.city.takasaki.gunma.jp/docs/2013121700122/ |website=www.city.takasaki.gunma.jp |publisher=高崎市公式HP |access-date=6 June 2022}}</ref>
While still working as an assistant director, [[Akira Kurosawa]] wrote a script called ''[[List of works by Akira Kurosawa|A German at Daruma Temple]]'' that used Shorinzan as the setting. It was written sometime between the 1930s to 1940s but was never filmed.
In 1947, [[Jomo Karuta]] was created; it features Shorinzan as the 'e' card.
== Cultural properties == Shorinzan is connected with two [[Cultural Property (Japan)|designated cultural properties]].<ref name="HP1" />
On the prefectural level, Senshin-tei (洗心亭). It was built in Taisho era and the size of 6x4 tatami mats. This is the building where Bruno Taut lived during his stay in Takasaki.
On the municipal level, the Daruma Daishi (Bodhidharma) statue (達磨大師立像).

On the municipal level, the Daruma Daishi (Bodhidharma) statue (達磨大師立像).

revision.text:
{{Short description|Buddhist temple in Honshu, Japan}} '''Shorinzan Daruma Temple''' (少林山達磨寺, shōrinzan daruma-ji) is a small temple located in [[Takasaki]], [[Gunma Prefecture]]. It was built in 1697 by the [[Obaku (school of Buddhism)|Obaku]] school of Zen Buddhism. Takasaki is known as the birthplace of [[Daruma doll|daruma]], as it is believed that the dolls originated from the region many years ago. The daruma of Shorinzan are said to be especially lucky, leading the city's PR department to dub Takasaki as The Lucky Town.<ref>{{cite web |title=The Lucky Town TAKASAKI |url=http://excitingcity-takasaki.com/ltt/index.html |website=excitingcity-takasaki.com |publisher=高崎市公式HP |access-date=6 June 2022}}</ref>
== Origin == An old legend states that sometime between 1673 and 1681, a heavy rain came and flooded the Usui River. Afterwards, villagers found a glowing, scented tree near the riverbed. They decided it was sacred and placed it inside an old hut that housed the local Kannon statue. As they did, purple haze began swirling around them.<ref name="DH1">{{cite web |title=【公式】黄檗宗 少林山達磨寺 |url=https://www.daruma.or.jp/ |website=www.daruma.or.jp |access-date=6 June 2022 |language=ja}}</ref>
Around the time of the flood, Bodhidharma Daishi appeared before a traveling monk named Ichiryo Koji in a dream. Allegedly he commanded Ichiryo Koji to carve his likeness out of a tree that was exactly the height of his nose. In 1680 Ichiryo Koji began his search for that tree, finally finding it when he visited the village that had once flooded. Ichiryo Koji carved the Bodhidharma Daishi's likeness with great devotion and care; it was officially placed next to the Kannon statue, both enshrined together.<ref name="DH1" />
Ichiryo Koji's devotion inspired the local villagers, and after that the area became known as "Shorinzan", the sacred place where the Daruma appeared. The ruling lord of the time then had Shorinzan Daruma Temple founded in 1697 in order to protect his castle (historically, Maebashi Castle, but at that time known by another name).<ref name="DH1" /><ref name="WHG1">{{cite web |title=七草大祭だるま市｜2022nd year 1月6-7日にだるま発祥の地少林山達磨寺で開催【高崎市鼻高町】 |url=https://we-love.gunma.jp/area/daruma |website=群馬の観光・旅行情報サイト We love群馬 |access-date=6 June 2022 |language=ja}}</ref>
== History == Originally Shorinzan was founded as a temple for the [[Sōtō|Soto]] school of Zen Buddhism, but in Meiji era was converted to the Obaku sect.
From 1783 to 1787, the 9th generation Zen Master of the temple aided suffering locals during the Great Tenmei famine. He taught them how to make papier-mâché dolls based on daruma dolls. This began what is now known as the Shorinzan Nanakusa Taisai Daruma Market Festival. It is held annually on January 6 and 7. In recent years it has attracted thousands of visitors each year who come to buy new daruma dolls and have their old ones burned.<ref name="WHG1" /><ref>{{cite web |title=Shorinzan Nanakusa Taisai Daruma Market Festival {{!}} Gunma Official Tourist Guide |url=https://www.visit-gunma.jp/en/spots/shorinzan-nanakusa-taisai-daruma-market-festival/ |website=visit-gunma.jp |publisher=Gunma Prefectural Government |access-date=6 June 2022}}</ref>
On June 14, 1881 Shorinzan burned down in a fire, losing important historical documents such as the details of its construction. There used to be a bell that was donated during World War II that was said to have an inscription inside dating it to 1712.
In 1934 German architect [[Bruno Taut]] fled from Nazi Germany with his wife. He was invited to Japan and eventually to Takasaki. There was a small empty building available on the outskirts of Shorinzan where they lived for the two years they spent in Takasaki. At his farewell party held on October 10, 1936, Taut expressed his desire to have his bones buried at the temple after his death.<ref name="DH1" /><ref name="HP1">{{cite web |title=洗心亭 |url=http://www.city.takasaki.gunma.jp/docs/2013121700122/ |website=www.city.takasaki.gunma.jp |publisher=高崎市公式HP |access-date=6 June 2022}}</ref>
While still working as an assistant director, [[Akira Kurosawa]] wrote a script called ''[[List of works by Akira Kurosawa|A German at Daruma Temple]]'' that used Shorinzan as the setting. It was written sometime between the 1930s to 1940s but was never filmed.
In 1947, [[Jomo Karuta]] was created; it features Shorinzan as the 'e' card.
== Cultural properties == Shorinzan is connected with two [[Cultural Property (Japan)|designated cultural properties]].<ref name="HP1" />
On the prefectural level, Senshin-tei (洗心亭). It was built in Taisho era and the size of 6x4 tatami mats. This is the building where Bruno Taut lived during his stay in Takasaki.
On the municipal level, the Daruma Daishi (Bodhidharma) statue (達磨大師立像).
==References== {{reflist}}

It was built in 1697 by the [[Obaku (school of Buddhism)|Obaku]] school of Zen Buddhism. Takasaki is known as the birthplace of [[Daruma doll|daruma]], as it is believed that the dolls originated from the region many years ago. The daruma of Shorinzan are said to be especially lucky, leading the city's PR department to dub Takasaki as The Lucky Town.<ref>{{cite web |title=The Lucky Town TAKASAKI |url=http://excitingcity-takasaki.com/ltt/index.html |website=excitingcity-takasaki.com |publisher=高崎市公式HP |access-date=6 June 2022}}</ref>
== Origin == An old legend states that sometime between 1673 and 1681, a heavy rain came and flooded the Usui River. Afterwards, villagers found a glowing, scented tree near the riverbed. They decided it was sacred and placed it inside an old hut that housed the local Kannon statue. In 1680 Ichiryo Koji began his search for that tree, finally finding it when he visited the village that had once flooded. Ichiryo Koji carved the Bodhidharma Daishi's likeness with great devotion and care; it was officially placed next to the Kannon statue, both enshrined together.<ref name="DH1" />

The ruling lord of the time then had Shorinzan Daruma Temple founded in 1697 in order to protect his castle (historically, Maebashi Castle, but at that time known by another name).<ref name="DH1" /><ref name="WHG1">{{cite web |title=七草大祭だるま市｜2022nd year 1月6-7日にだるま発祥の地少林山達磨寺で開催【高崎市鼻高町】 |url=https://we-love.gunma.jp/area/daruma |website=群馬の観光・旅行情報サイト We love群馬 |access-date=6 June 2022 |language=ja}}</ref>
== History == Originally Shorinzan was founded as a temple for the [[Sōtō|Soto]] school of Zen Buddhism, but in Meiji era was converted to the Obaku sect.
From 1783 to 1787, the 9th generation Zen Master of the temple aided suffering locals during the Great Tenmei famine. He taught them how to make papier-mâché dolls based on daruma dolls. This began what is now known as the Shorinzan Nanakusa Taisai Daruma Market Festival. It is held annually on January 6 and 7. In recent years it has attracted thousands of visitors each year who come to buy new daruma dolls and have their old ones burned.<ref name="WHG1" /><ref>{{cite web |title=Shorinzan Nanakusa Taisai Daruma Market Festival {{!}} Gunma Official Tourist Guide |url=https://www.visit-gunma.jp/en/spots/shorinzan-nanakusa-taisai-daruma-market-festival/ |website=visit-gunma.jp |publisher=Gunma Prefectural Government |access-date=6 June 2022}}</ref>
On June 14, 1881 Shorinzan burned down in a fire, losing important historical documents such as the details of its construction. There used to be a bell that was donated during World War II that was said to have an inscription inside dating it to 1712.
In 1934 German architect [[Bruno Taut]] fled from Nazi Germany with his wife. He was invited to Japan and eventually to Takasaki. At his farewell party held on October 10, 1936, Taut expressed his desire to have his bones buried at the temple after his death.<ref name="DH1" /><ref name="HP1">{{cite web |title=洗心亭 |url=http://www.city.takasaki.gunma.jp/docs/2013121700122/ |website=www.city.takasaki.gunma.jp |publisher=高崎市公式HP |access-date=6 June 2022}}</ref>
While still working as an assistant director, [[Akira Kurosawa]] wrote a script called ''[[List of works by Akira Kurosawa|A German at Daruma Temple]]'' that used Shorinzan as the setting. It was written sometime between the 1930s to 1940s but was never filmed.
In 1947, [[Jomo Karuta]] was created; it features Shorinzan as the 'e' card.

On the prefectural level, Senshin-tei (洗心亭). It was built in Taisho era and the size of 6x4 tatami mats. This is the building where Bruno Taut lived during his stay in Takasaki.
On the municipal level, the Daruma Daishi (Bodhidharma) statue (達磨大師立像).

In 1934 German architect [[Bruno Taut]] fled from Nazi Germany with his wife. He was invited to Japan and eventually to Takasaki. There was a small empty building available on the outskirts of Shorinzan where they lived for the two years they spent in Takasaki. At his farewell party held on October 10, 1936, Taut expressed his desire to have his bones buried at the temple after his death.<ref name="DH1" /><ref name="HP1">{{cite web |title=洗心亭 |url=http://www.city.takasaki.gunma.jp/docs/2013121700122/ |website=www.city.takasaki.gunma.jp |publisher=高崎市公式HP |access-date=6 June 2022}}</ref>
While still working as an assistant director, [[Akira Kurosawa]] wrote a script called ''[[List of works by Akira Kurosawa|A German at Daruma Temple]]'' that used Shorinzan as the setting. It was written sometime between the 1930s to 1940s but was never filmed.
In 1947, [[Jomo Karuta]] was created; it features Shorinzan as the 'e' card.
== Cultural properties == Shorinzan is connected with two [[Cultural Property (Japan)|designated cultural properties]].<ref name="HP1" />
On the prefectural level, Senshin-tei (洗心亭). It was built in Taisho era and the size of 6x4 tatami mats. This is the building where Bruno Taut lived during his stay in Takasaki.
On the municipal level, the Daruma Daishi (Bodhidharma) statue (達磨大師立像).
==References== {{reflist}}

{{draft categories| [[Category:Religious buildings and structures in Gunma Prefecture]] [[Category:Designated historic sites of Gunma Prefecture]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 3]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:11]] }} [[sn:Shorinzan Daruma Temple]] [[en:Shorinzan Daruma Temple]]

{{Short description|Buddhist temple in Honshu, Japan}} '''Shorinzan Daruma Temple''' (少林山達磨寺, shōrinzan daruma-ji) is a small temple located in [[Takasaki]], [[Gunma Prefecture]]. It was built in 1697 by the [[Obaku (school of Buddhism)|Obaku]] school of Zen Buddhism. Takasaki is known as the birthplace of [[Daruma doll|daruma]], as it is believed that the dolls originated from the region many years ago. The daruma of Shorinzan are said to be especially lucky, leading the city's PR department to dub Takasaki as The Lucky Town.<ref>{{cite web |title=The Lucky Town TAKASAKI |url=http://excitingcity-takasaki.com/ltt/index.html |website=excitingcity-takasaki.com |publisher=高崎市公式HP |access-date=6 June 2022}}</ref>
== Origin == An old legend states that sometime between 1673 and 1681, a heavy rain came and flooded the Usui River. Afterwards, villagers found a glowing, scented tree near the riverbed. They decided it was sacred and placed it inside an old hut that housed the local Kannon statue. As they did, purple haze began swirling around them.<ref name="DH1">{{cite web |title=【公式】黄檗宗 少林山達磨寺 |url=https://www.daruma.or.jp/ |website=www.daruma.or.jp |access-date=6 June 2022 |language=ja}}</ref>
Around the time of the flood, Bodhidharma Daishi appeared before a traveling monk named Ichiryo Koji in a dream. Allegedly he commanded Ichiryo Koji to carve his likeness out of a tree that was exactly the height of his nose. In 1680 Ichiryo Koji began his search for that tree, finally finding it when he visited the village that had once flooded. Ichiryo Koji carved the Bodhidharma Daishi's likeness with great devotion and care; it was officially placed next to the Kannon statue, both enshrined together.<ref name="DH1" />
Ichiryo Koji's devotion inspired the local villagers, and after that the area became known as "Shorinzan", the sacred place where the Daruma appeared. The ruling lord of the time then had Shorinzan Daruma Temple founded in 1697 in order to protect his castle (historically, Maebashi Castle, but at that time known by another name).<ref name="DH1" /><ref name="WHG1">{{cite web |title=七草大祭だるま市｜2022nd year 1月6-7日にだるま発祥の地少林山達磨寺で開催【高崎市鼻高町】 |url=https://we-love.gunma.jp/area/daruma |website=群馬の観光・旅行情報サイト We love群馬 |access-date=6 June 2022 |language=ja}}</ref>
== History == Originally Shorinzan was founded as a temple for the [[Sōtō|Soto]] school of Zen Buddhism, but in Meiji era was converted to the Obaku sect.
From 1783 to 1787, the 9th generation Zen Master of the temple aided suffering locals during the Great Tenmei famine. He taught them how to make papier-mâché dolls based on daruma dolls. This began what is now known as the Shorinzan Nanakusa Taisai Daruma Market Festival. It is held annually on January 6 and 7. In recent years it has attracted thousands of visitors each year who come to buy new daruma dolls and have their old ones burned.<ref name="WHG1" /><ref>{{cite web |title=Shorinzan Nanakusa Taisai Daruma Market Festival {{!}} Gunma Official Tourist Guide |url=https://www.visit-gunma.jp/en/spots/shorinzan-nanakusa-taisai-daruma-market-festival/ |website=visit-gunma.jp |publisher=Gunma Prefectural Government |access-date=6 June 2022}}</ref>
On June 14, 1881 Shorinzan burned down in a fire, losing important historical documents such as the details of its construction. There used to be a bell that was donated during World War II that was said to have an inscription inside dating it to 1712.
In 1934 German architect [[Bruno Taut]] fled from Nazi Germany with his wife. He was invited to Japan and eventually to Takasaki. There was a small empty building available on the outskirts of Shorinzan where they lived for the two years they spent in Takasaki. At his farewell party held on October 10, 1936, Taut expressed his desire to have his bones buried at the temple after his death.<ref name="DH1" /><ref name="HP1">{{cite web |title=洗心亭 |url=http://www.city.takasaki.gunma.jp/docs/2013121700122/ |website=www.city.takasaki.gunma.jp |publisher=高崎市公式HP |access-date=6 June 2022}}</ref>
While still working as an assistant director, [[Akira Kurosawa]] wrote a script called ''[[List of works by Akira Kurosawa|A German at Daruma Temple]]'' that used Shorinzan as the setting. It was written sometime between the 1930s to 1940s but was never filmed.
In 1947, [[Jomo Karuta]] was created; it features Shorinzan as the 'e' card.
== Cultural properties == Shorinzan is connected with two [[Cultural Property (Japan)|designated cultural properties]].<ref name="HP1" />
On the prefectural level, Senshin-tei (洗心亭). It was built in Taisho era and the size of 6x4 tatami mats. This is the building where Bruno Taut lived during his stay in Takasaki.
On the municipal level, the Daruma Daishi (Bodhidharma) statue (達磨大師立像).
==References== {{reflist}}

This began what is now known as the Shorinzan Nanakusa Taisai Daruma Market Festival. It is held annually on January 6 and 7. In recent years it has attracted thousands of visitors each year who come to buy new daruma dolls and have their old ones burned.<ref name="WHG1" /><ref>{{cite web |title=Shorinzan Nanakusa Taisai Daruma Market Festival {{!}} Gunma Official Tourist Guide |url=https://www.visit-gunma.jp/en/spots/shorinzan-nanakusa-taisai-daruma-market-festival/ |website=visit-gunma.jp |publisher=Gunma Prefectural Government |access-date=6 June 2022}}</ref>
On June 14, 1881 Shorinzan burned down in a fire, losing important historical documents such as the details of its construction. There used to be a bell that was donated during World War II that was said to have an inscription inside dating it to 1712.
In 1934 German architect [[Bruno Taut]] fled from Nazi Germany with his wife. He was invited to Japan and eventually to Takasaki. There was a small empty building available on the outskirts of Shorinzan where they lived for the two years they spent in Takasaki. At his farewell party held on October 10, 1936, Taut expressed his desire to have his bones buried at the temple after his death.<ref name="DH1" /><ref name="HP1">{{cite web |title=洗心亭 |url=http://www.city.takasaki.gunma.jp/docs/2013121700122/ |website=www.city.takasaki.gunma.jp |publisher=高崎市公式HP |access-date=6 June 2022}}</ref>
While still working as an assistant director, [[Akira Kurosawa]] wrote a script called ''[[List of works by Akira Kurosawa|A German at Daruma Temple]]'' that used Shorinzan as the setting. It was written sometime between the 1930s to 1940s but was never filmed.
In 1947, [[Jomo Karuta]] was created; it features Shorinzan as the 'e' card.
== Cultural properties == Shorinzan is connected with two [[Cultural Property (Japan)|designated cultural properties]].<ref name="HP1" />
On the prefectural level, Senshin-tei (洗心亭). It was built in Taisho era and the size of 6x4 tatami mats. This is the building where Bruno Taut lived during his stay in Takasaki.
On the municipal level, the Daruma Daishi (Bodhidharma) statue (達磨大師立像).

[[sn:Shorinzan Daruma Temple]] [[en:Shorinzan Daruma Temple]]

{{Short description|Buddhist temple in Honshu, Japan}} '''Shorinzan Daruma Temple''' (少林山達磨寺, shōrinzan daruma-ji) is a small temple located in [[Takasaki]], [[Gunma Prefecture]]. It was built in 1697 by the [[Obaku (school of Buddhism)|Obaku]] school of Zen Buddhism. Takasaki is known as the birthplace of [[Daruma doll|daruma]], as it is believed that the dolls originated from the region many years ago. The daruma of Shorinzan are said to be especially lucky, leading the city's PR department to dub Takasaki as The Lucky Town.<ref>{{cite web |title=The Lucky Town TAKASAKI |url=http://excitingcity-takasaki.com/ltt/index.html |website=excitingcity-takasaki.com |publisher=高崎市公式HP |access-date=6 June 2022}}</ref>
== Origin == An old legend states that sometime between 1673 and 1681, a heavy rain came and flooded the Usui River. Afterwards, villagers found a glowing, scented tree near the riverbed. They decided it was sacred and placed it inside an old hut that housed the local Kannon statue. As they did, purple haze began swirling around them.<ref name="DH1">{{cite web |title=【公式】黄檗宗 少林山達磨寺 |url=https://www.daruma.or.jp/ |website=www.daruma.or.jp |access-date=6 June 2022 |language=ja}}</ref>
Around the time of the flood, Bodhidharma Daishi appeared before a traveling monk named Ichiryo Koji in a dream. Allegedly he commanded Ichiryo Koji to carve his likeness out of a tree that was exactly the height of his nose. In 1680 Ichiryo Koji began his search for that tree, finally finding it when he visited the village that had once flooded. Ichiryo Koji carved the Bodhidharma Daishi's likeness with great devotion and care; it was officially placed next to the Kannon statue, both enshrined together.<ref name="DH1" />
Ichiryo Koji's devotion inspired the local villagers, and after that the area became known as "Shorinzan", the sacred place where the Daruma appeared. The ruling lord of the time then had Shorinzan Daruma Temple founded in 1697 in order to protect his castle (historically, Maebashi Castle, but at that time known by another name).<ref name="DH1" /><ref name="WHG1">{{cite web |title=七草大祭だるま市｜2022nd year 1月6-7日にだるま発祥の地少林山達磨寺で開催【高崎市鼻高町】 |url=https://we-love.gunma.jp/area/daruma |website=群馬の観光・旅行情報サイト We love群馬 |access-date=6 June 2022 |language=ja}}</ref>
== History == Originally Shorinzan was founded as a temple for the [[Sōtō|Soto]] school of Zen Buddhism, but in Meiji era was converted to the Obaku sect.
From 1783 to 1787, the 9th generation Zen Master of the temple aided suffering locals during the Great Tenmei famine. He taught them how to make papier-mâché dolls based on daruma dolls. This began what is now known as the Shorinzan Nanakusa Taisai Daruma Market Festival. It is held annually on January 6 and 7. In recent years it has attracted thousands of visitors each year who come to buy new daruma dolls and have their old ones burned.<ref name="WHG1" /><ref>{{cite web |title=Shorinzan Nanakusa Taisai Daruma Market Festival {{!}} Gunma Official Tourist Guide |url=https://www.visit-gunma.jp/en/spots/shorinzan-nanakusa-taisai-daruma-market-festival/ |website=visit-gunma.jp |publisher=Gunma Prefectural Government |access-date=6 June 2022}}</ref>
On June 14, 1881 Shorinzan burned down in a fire, losing important historical documents such as the details of its construction. There used to be a bell that was donated during World War II that was said to have an inscription inside dating it to 1712.
In 1934 German architect [[Bruno Taut]] fled from Nazi Germany with his wife. He was invited to Japan and eventually to Takasaki. There was a small empty building available on the outskirts of Shorinzan where they lived for the two years they spent in Takasaki. At his farewell party held on October 10, 1936, Taut expressed his desire to have his bones buried at the temple after his death.<ref name="DH1" /><ref name="HP1">{{cite web |title=洗心亭 |url=http://www.city.takasaki.gunma.jp/docs/2013121700122/ |website=www.city.takasaki.gunma.jp |publisher=高崎市公式HP |access-date=6 June 2022}}</ref>
While still working as an assistant director, [[Akira Kurosawa]] wrote a script called ''[[List of works by Akira Kurosawa|A German at Daruma Temple]]'' that used Shorinzan as the setting. It was written sometime between the 1930s to 1940s but was never filmed.
In 1947, [[Jomo Karuta]] was created; it features Shorinzan as the 'e' card.
== Cultural properties == Shorinzan is connected with two [[Cultural Property (Japan)|designated cultural properties]].<ref name="HP1" />
On the prefectural level, Senshin-tei (洗心亭). It was built in Taisho era and the size of 6x4 tatami mats. This is the building where Bruno Taut lived during his stay in Takasaki.
On the municipal level, the Daruma Daishi (Bodhidharma) statue (達磨大師立像).
